channel.title: Assignments on OOFP 2025-26
channel.description: Recent content in Assignments on OOFP 2025-26

item.title: Assignment 1
item.description:
Assignment 1: higher-order functions and linear recursion #

Hint. In this assignment, you can often take advantage of the native higher-order functions that we have encountered: filter, map, all, any, etc.

Exercise 1: utility functions #

Implement the functions below by completing the function stubs in the file Src/Utility.hs.

Hint. Some of these functions can be used to implement others.

Hint. Feel free to create additional (auxiliary) functions to simplify your implementation.

item.title: Assignment 2
item.description:
Assignment 2: functional patterns #

Exercise 1: utility functions #

Implement the functions below by completing the function stubs in the file Src/Utility.hs.

Hint. Some of these functions can be used to implement others.

Hint. Feel free to create additional (auxiliary) functions to simplify your implementation.

lastMatch #

Implement the function

lastMatch :: (a -> Bool) -> [a] -> Maybe a

such that lastMatch f xs is:

  • Just x if x is the last element of the list xs for which f x is True, or
  • Nothing if there is no such x.

For instance,

item.title: Assignment 3
item.description:
Assignment 3: inheritance #

Exercise 1: colored lists #

We consider lists that:

  • consist of green and red nodes, where
  • each node is labeled with an integer, and
  • the head node is green.

We model such lists with the following classes and interfaces:

Restriction. You can assume that lists are acyclic, i.e. no node points to one of its ancestors.

Complete or modify the abstract class AbstractNode and the classes RedNode, GreenNode and HeadNodeImpl in the folder ex1, so that:

item.title: Assignment 4
item.description:
Assignment 4: abstract data types #

Exercise 1: board game #

We model a simple board game with rooms and players.

Each room has:

  • a set of adjacent rooms, and
  • a set of players present in the room.

Each player has

  • a type (paper, rock or scissor), and
  • a number of health points.

An action consists in moving one player from a room to another (adjacent) room:

  • When the player enters the room, it may deal one point of damage to each other player that is already in the room (based on their rock/paper/scissor types), and receive damage in the same way.
  • After all these operations have been performed, if a player has no more health points, then it should be removed from the board.

Complete the class BoardImpl so that it correctly implements the interface Board. The expected behavior of each method is described in the interface.

item.title: Assignment 5
item.description:
Assignment 5: binary recursion and Java streams #

Exercise 1: algebraic expressions #

Conventions. In this exercise, an algebraic expression is either:

  • an integer, or
  • an operation, i.e. an operator (+, * or /) with two operands.

The operator / stands for integer division, meaning that the result is rounded down.

Example. The expression 7 / 3 evaluates to 2.

We represent an algebraic expression as an instance of the Java interface Expression in the following diagram:
